transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Sarah. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been feeling a bit uneasy lately. It’s just that sometimes I don’t hear from Amelia throughout the day, and it really gets to me. I start worrying about all sorts of things. It’s hard to shake off those thoughts.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Thank you for sharing that, Sarah. It sounds like not hearing from Amelia brings up a lot of unease and worry for you. Can you tell me more about the thoughts that come up when you don’t hear from her?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s like my mind jumps to the worst-case scenarios. I think maybe something bad has happened to her or that she doesn’t care enough to stay in touch with me. Sometimes I even wonder if she still loves me. It’s just... really hard to not feel anxious when I’m in that space.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like when you don’t hear from Amelia, your mind starts racing with some very intense worries about her safety and your relationship. That must be really challenging to navigate. How do those thoughts and feelings affect you throughout the day?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It’s really tough. When I don’t hear from her, I feel this knot in my stomach, and I can’t relax at all. I just keep checking my phone, hoping for a message. I end up texting her a lot, and I know it probably comes off as needy or clingy, but I can’t help it. I just feel so unsettled until I hear from her. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop of worry.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That knot in your stomach sounds really uncomfortable, and it makes sense that you would want to reach out to her to ease that feeling. It seems like the worry creates a cycle where you feel unsettled until you get a response, which leads to more texting. I wonder if you’ve noticed any patterns or triggers that make these feelings stronger for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I think there are definitely some patterns. It usually happens when I haven’t heard from her for a while, especially if it’s during a busy day or if I know she has a lot going on. I start imagining all these scenarios in my head, and it just spirals from there. I guess when I feel disconnected, it amplifies my worries. It’s like my mind just takes over, and I can’t seem to stop it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like those moments of not hearing from her, especially during busy times, really heighten your sense of disconnection and worry. It’s understandable that your mind would start to fill in the gaps with those scenarios. When you say your mind takes over, what do you think it’s trying to protect you from in those moments?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s trying to protect me from feeling abandoned or alone. I’ve always had this fear of being left behind, you know? It’s like my mind is trying to prepare me for the worst, so I’m not caught off guard. But instead, it just makes everything feel worse. I end up feeling even more anxious and sad, like I’m just waiting for something bad to happen. It’s exhausting.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That fear of being abandoned sounds really deep-rooted and understandably difficult to carry. It seems like your mind is trying to shield you from that pain, but it ends up creating more anxiety instead. It must be exhausting to feel like you’re constantly on guard. Have you noticed any moments or experiences in your past that might have contributed to that fear of being left behind?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I think it goes back to my childhood. I had a tough time when I was separated from my parents, and it really affected me. I remember feeling so alone and scared when they weren’t around. Those feelings have stuck with me, and I guess they’ve shaped how I react in relationships now. It’s like I’m always worried that if someone isn’t there, they might not come back. It’s hard to shake that feeling, even as an adult.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that experience from your childhood has had a lasting impact on you, creating a strong association between separation and fear. Feeling alone and scared during those times must have been really tough, and it makes sense that those feelings would carry into your adult relationships. It’s understandable that you would want to protect yourself from that kind of pain. How do you feel about recognizing that connection between your past and your current feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It’s a bit of a relief to talk about it, honestly. I guess I’ve always known on some level that my past influences how I feel now, but hearing it out loud makes it feel more real. It’s like I can see the pattern more clearly. At the same time, it’s kind of overwhelming to think about how deep those feelings run. I want to be able to trust and feel secure, but it’s hard when that fear is always lurking in the background.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m glad to hear that talking about it feels like a relief, even if it’s also overwhelming. It’s a big step to recognize those patterns and how deeply rooted they are. Wanting to trust and feel secure is completely natural, especially when those fears are so present. What do you think might help you start to build that sense of trust and security, both in yourself and in your relationship with Amelia?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it might help to communicate more openly with Amelia about how I feel. I’ve been holding back because I don’t want to seem needy or like I’m putting too much pressure on her. But maybe if I could share some of my worries, it would help me feel more connected and understood. I also wonder if finding ways to manage my anxiety when I don’t hear from her could be beneficial. I just need to figure out how to do that without feeling like I’m falling apart.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re considering some really important steps, like opening up to Amelia about your feelings and finding ways to manage your anxiety. Sharing your worries could help strengthen your connection and help her understand where you’re coming from. It’s also great that you’re thinking about ways to cope with the anxiety when you don’t hear from her. What kinds of strategies or practices have you thought about trying to manage that anxiety?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’ve thought about maybe trying some mindfulness techniques or deep breathing exercises to help calm myself down when those anxious thoughts start to creep in. Sometimes I go for a run, which helps clear my mind, but I know I need something I can do in the moment when I’m feeling really anxious. Journaling could also be a way to express my feelings without overwhelming Amelia. I just need to find a way to remind myself that it’s okay to feel this way and that I can manage it without relying solely on her for reassurance.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like thoughtful and proactive strategies, Sarah. Mindfulness techniques, deep breathing, and even journaling could provide you with some valuable tools to help ground yourself in those anxious moments. It’s also encouraging to hear that you recognize the importance of managing your feelings independently. Finding that balance between seeking support from Amelia and being able to self-soothe is a significant step. How do you feel about starting to incorporate some of these practices into your routine?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit hopeful about it, actually. It seems like a good way to take some control over my anxiety instead of letting it control me. I know it won’t be easy, and I might struggle at first, but having those tools in my back pocket could really help. I just need to remind myself that it’s okay to take small steps and that it’s a process. I think if I can start with just one or two of these practices, it might make a difference in how I handle things when I’m feeling anxious.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling hopeful and ready to take those small steps. Acknowledging that it’s a process and that it’s okay to start small is such an important mindset. It sounds like you’re setting yourself up for success by being gentle with yourself as you explore these practices. What’s one small step you feel ready to take first?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’d like to start with some deep breathing exercises. They seem manageable, and I can do them anywhere, even when I’m feeling really anxious. I can set aside a few minutes each day to practice, maybe even before I go to bed or when I first wake up. Just having that little moment to focus on my breath might help me feel a bit more grounded. I’ll see how it goes and then maybe add in some journaling after that.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a great plan, Sarah. Deep breathing exercises can be a really effective way to create a sense of calm and grounding, especially in those anxious moments. Setting aside time for it in the morning or before bed could help establish a routine that feels supportive. It’s also encouraging that you’re open to adding journaling later on. How do you feel about checking in with yourself after a week or so to see how the deep breathing practice is going?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think that sounds like a good idea. Checking in with myself after a week would give me a chance to reflect on how the deep breathing is helping or if I need to adjust anything. It might also help me stay accountable to actually doing it. I can see if I’m feeling any different or if it’s making a positive impact on my anxiety. I’ll try to be honest with myself about how it’s going, even if it’s just a small change.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a really constructive approach, Sarah. Reflecting on your experience and staying accountable to yourself can be very empowering. It’s great that you’re committed to being honest about your feelings, no matter how small the changes may seem. Remember, every step counts. Is there anything else you’d like to explore or discuss today before we wrap up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’ve shared a lot today, and I appreciate you listening. I feel like I have some good ideas to work on moving forward. I guess I just want to make sure I keep reminding myself that it’s okay to feel anxious and that I’m not alone in this. I’m grateful for the space to talk about it. Thank you for your support.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you found our conversation helpful, and I appreciate you sharing so openly. It’s important to remind yourself that feeling anxious is okay and that you’re not alone in this journey. You’ve taken some meaningful steps today, and I hope you continue to feel that sense of hope as you move forward. Thank you for trusting me with your thoughts and feelings. If you ever want to talk again or explore anything further, I’m here for you. Take care, Sarah.

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much. I really appreciate your support and understanding. It feels good to have a space to talk about these things. I’ll definitely keep working on my breathing exercises and check in with myself. I’ll take care, and I hope to talk again soon.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Sarah. I’m glad you found this space helpful, and I look forward to hearing how your breathing exercises go when we talk again. Take care of yourself, and remember to be gentle with yourself as you navigate this process. I’ll be here whenever you’re ready to chat again. Wishing you all the best!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I’ll keep that in mind and try to be gentle with myself. I appreciate your encouragement and support. I’ll look forward to our next conversation. Take care!
